title: Acyclicity
lean_code_with_comments:
def IsAcyclic (G : CDMG Node) : Prop :=
  ‚ąÄ v ‚ąą G, ¬¨ ‚ąÉ p : Walk G v v, p.IsDirectedWalk ‚ąß 0 < p.length
lean_code_without_comments:
def IsAcyclic (G : CDMG Node) : Prop :=
  ‚ąÄ v ‚ąą G, ¬¨ ‚ąÉ p : Walk G v v, p.IsDirectedWalk ‚ąß 0 < p.length
lean_explanation:
The definition `IsAcyclic` lives field-style on `CDMG`, so it is invoked via dot-notation as `G.IsAcyclic`. The single parameter `G : CDMG Node` is the graph, and `Node : Type*` is the ambient vertex type ‚ÄĒ a type-level placeholder that subsumes whatever carrier the user picked. The body is a single closed proposition: $\forall v \in G,\ \neg \exists\, p, \ldots$.

The bounded-quantifier syntax `‚ąÄ v ‚ąą G, ‚Ä¶` is Lean shorthand for `‚ąÄ v, v ‚ąą G ‚Üí ‚Ä¶`. The membership `v ‚ąą G` is not membership in a `Set` ‚ÄĒ it routes through the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance set up in `def_3_2`, which unfolds to `v ‚ąą G.J ‚ą™ G.V`. So the quantifier ranges over the disjoint union of input nodes ($J$) and output nodes ($V$), matching the LN's literal phrase "for any node $v \in G$".

The existential then says: there is a walk `p : Walk G v v` from $v$ back to itself that is (i) directed at every step (`p.IsDirectedWalk`) and (ii) traverses at least one edge (`0 < p.length`). `Walk G v v` is the chapter-3 inductive walk type from `Walks.lean`, parametrised by source and target vertices; `IsDirectedWalk` is the predicate that every constructor in `p` is a `.forward` arrow (rather than a bidirected or reverse step); `length` counts the number of `cons` constructors, so `0 < p.length` excludes the trivial single-vertex walk `Walk.nil hv`.

The surrounding `¬¨ ‚ąÉ` is then the literal Lean transcription of the LN's "there does not exist any non-trivial directed walk". Logically `¬¨ ‚ąÉ p, P p` is equivalent to `‚ąÄ p, ¬¨ P p`; downstream consumers that prefer the universal form get there with a single `rw [not_exists]`.
design_choices:
**The predicate lives in the `CDMG` namespace.** Acyclicity is a property of the graph ‚ÄĒ it inspects only $G$'s edges and vertices ‚ÄĒ so it is exposed as `G.IsAcyclic` via dot-notation, parallel to the structure fields `G.J`, `G.V`, `G.E`, `G.L`. Walk-level predicates like `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` live one level higher (under `Causality`) because they characterise walks rather than graphs.

**The quantifier is `‚ąÄ v ‚ąą G`, not `‚ąÄ v : Node` or `‚ąÄ v ‚ąą G.V`.** The literal LN phrase is "for any node $v \in G$". Quantifying over the ambient type `Node` would include vertices outside $G$ and obscure the LN-faithfulness; restricting to `G.V` would silently drop the $J$-half. The bounded form `‚ąÄ v ‚ąą G` routes through the `Membership` instance and ranges over $J \cup V$, exactly the LN reading. The $J$-half is automatically vacuous (no directed edge can land at a $J$-node by `hE_subset`), so no expressive power is lost.

**The shape is `¬¨ ‚ąÉ p, ‚Ä¶` rather than `‚ąÄ p, ¬¨ ‚Ä¶`.** Logically equivalent, but `¬¨ ‚ąÉ` mirrors the LN's surface phrasing "there does not exist". This differs from Mathlib's `SimpleGraph.IsAcyclic`, which uses the `‚ąÄ ‚Ä¶ ¬¨` shape; the structural mismatch is unavoidable because `SimpleGraph` has neither the $J/V$ split nor a bidirected channel.

**The non-triviality clause `0 < p.length` is mandatory.** Without it, the trivial walk `Walk.nil hv : Walk G v v` would vacuously witness `p.IsDirectedWalk` (since there are no edges to check), forcing every non-empty CDMG to fail acyclicity. The LN sidesteps this with the word "non-trivial"; the operator addition pins it down as "at least one edge", which translates directly to `0 < p.length` since `Walk.length` counts edges.

**`def` rather than `abbrev`, `class`, or `Decidable` instance.** A plain `def` returning `Prop` gives acyclicity a stable name for downstream hypotheses. An `abbrev` would inline at every use site; a `class` would dispatch via typeclass inference, which is wrong for a contingent property of a specific $G$. No `Decidable` instance is provided ‚ÄĒ chapter-3 proofs do not need it, and `Classical.dec` covers the rare downstream case.

**Compose `Walk` + `IsDirectedWalk` + `length` rather than introduce a fresh `DirectedCycle` inductive.** A bespoke cycle type would force re-derivation of every walk-level lemma (`support`, `length`, `IsPath`, the bifurcation arms) on the new type. The chosen composition pays off downstream: each intervention operation (`do`, `split`, `swig`, marginalisation) needs a "preserves acyclicity" lemma, and these reduce uniformly to walk-correspondence statements rather than per-operation cycle translations.

**No Mathlib reuse.** `SimpleGraph.IsAcyclic` is undirected; `DiGraph` lacks the $J/V$ split and bidirected channel. Coercing to a Mathlib graph type would lose the structure the chapter-3 theory depends on.
